elements.sent-2411-001: In this article, we study quantum critical phenomena in surfaces of symmetry-protected topological matter, i.e. surface topological quantum criticality.
elements.sent-2411-002: A generic phase boundary of gapless surfaces in a symmetry-protected state shall be a co-dimension one manifold in an interaction parameter space of dimension $D_p$ (where $p$ refers to the parameter space) where the value of $D_p$ further depends on bulk topologies.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the context of fermionic topological insulators that we focus on, $D_p$ depends on the number of half-Dirac cones $\mathcal{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-004: We construct such manifolds explicitly for a few interaction parameter spaces with various $D_p$ values.
elements.sent-2411-005: Most importantly, we further illustrate that in cases with $D_p=3$ and $6$, there are sub-manifolds of fixed points that dictate the universalities of surface topological quantum criticality.
elements.sent-2411-006: These infrared stable manifolds are associated with emergent symmetries in the renormalization-group-equation flow naturally appearing in the loop expansion.
elements.sent-2411-007: Unlike in the usual order-disorder quantum critical phenomena, typically governed by an isolated Wilson-Fisher fixed point, we find in the one-loop approximation surface topological quantum criticalities are naturally captured by conformal manifolds where the number of marginal operators uniquely determines their co-dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-008: Isolated strong coupling fixed points also appear, usually as the endpoints in the phase boundary of surface topological quantum phases.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, their extreme infrared instabilities along multiple directions suggest that they shall be related to multi-critical surface topological quantum critical phenomena rather than generic surface topological quantum criticality.
elements.sent-2411-010: We also discuss and classify higher-loop symmetry-breaking effects, which can either distort the conformal manifolds or further break the conformal manifolds down to a few distinct fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-011: Introduction Symmetry-protected states (SPTs) \cite{kitaev(2009),wen(2009),Ryu(2010) are known to have robust and unique gapless boundary states that can lead to various potential applications.}
elements.sent-2411-012: One particularly fascinating example is the surface electronic states of 3D topological insulators\cite{fukane(2005),bernevigzhang(2006),bernevighugheszhang(2006),fukanemele(2007),moorebalents(2007),hasankanereview(2010), Qizhangreview(2011),bernevigbook(2013) which have been attracting much attention.}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the general paradigm of SPTs, these surface states usually remain gapless unless the protecting symmetry is explicitly broken by applied external fields\cite{shoucheng(2008),fukane(2008),zhang(2009) or spontaneously broken due to strong interactions, either attractive\cite{mudry(2010),imada(2011),imada(2012),sondhi(2013),herbut(2013),sarma(2013),Ponte(2014),Grover(2014),neupert(2015),maciejko(2016),maciejko(2021) or repulsive\cite{xu(2010),xu(2010)(2),tetsuya(2011),stern(2012),franz(2012),fritz(2013),potter(2015) .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Although the free fermion topological states and their gapless surfaces have been quite thoroughly understood, what happens when interactions, especially strong interactions, are present remains to be a fascinating topic.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, if the protecting symmetry is unbroken in the presence of strong interactions, the surface states can become gapped only when they are topologically ordered.
elements.sent-2411-016: When the protecting symmetries are time-reversal and U(1) symmetries, there shall be 8 classes of 3D topological distinct states when interactions are taken into account\cite{senthil(2015) .}
elements.sent-2411-017: If the protecting symmetry is time-reversal symmetry only, there shall be 8 classes in 1D and 16 classes in 3D\cite{Fidkowski(2010),Fidkowski(2011),fidkowski(2013) .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although it is unclear under which conditions or which practical Hamiltonians these exotic states can emerge in a condensed matter system\cite{metlitski(2015), song(2017) , the complexity of boundary quantum dynamics always plays a paramount role in our general understanding of SPTs including topological insulators, when strong interactions are present.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This article is focused on an interplay between topology and interactions, from a perspective of boundary quantum dynamics of topological states.
elements.sent-2411-020: We restrict ourselves to a topological insulator in a cubic crystal that is protected by the time-reversal symmetry, $U(1)$ symmetry and crystal symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-021: We will focus on surface fermions with attractive interactions.
elements.sent-2411-022: It is worth emphasizing that discussions on co-dimension one phase boundary manifolds in the space of interaction parameters are very generic and valid for other surface topological quantum criticality in SPTs.
elements.sent-2411-023: The results on the structure of conformal submanifolds are, on the other hand specific for topological insulators.
elements.sent-2411-025: Firstly, gapless surface fermions or electrons with attractive interactions can be crucial to the making of topological surface superconductivity as well as emergent Majorana fermions\cite{fukane(2008),SDShrma(2010) .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Such surface Majorana fermions can have potential applications for topological quantum computing.
elements.sent-2411-027: Secondly, the most commonly studied gapless states near a free fermion fixed point are very distinctly different from other gapless conformal field states near strong coupling fixed points studied below.
elements.sent-2411-028: It is essential to understand what possible universal classes of gapless states can be present as gapless boundaries of a topological state with inter-particle interactions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Thirdly and perhaps most importantly, we are going to identify the phase boundary of free fermion gapless states in interaction parameter spaces.
elements.sent-2411-030: A phase boundary that separates the gapless surface state from other distinct states of matter usually appears as a manifold embedded in a multidimensional space of interaction parameters.
elements.sent-2411-031: For a dimension $D_p$ interaction parameter space, the phase boundary generically is a $D_p-1$ dimension manifold, i.e. a co-dimension one manifold.
elements.sent-2411-032: Each point in the manifold represents a possible quantum critical point.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, there only appear to be a discrete number of universality classes that determine dynamics on all critical states situated in the phase boundary manifold.
elements.sent-2411-034: These universality classes can be identified with scale-conformal symmetric fixed points naturally emerging in the studies of standard renormalization group equations.
elements.sent-2411-035: In fact, the fixed points can also form a sub-manifold but with co-dimension equal to or higher than 2 but less than $D_p-1$ itself (See details below).
elements.sent-2411-036: Let us further elaborate on the second and third points.
elements.sent-2411-037: To answer the question of universality classes of gapless boundaries, technically one needs to identify all possible scale symmetric fixed points in surface dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-038: While a free fermion fixed point can be naturally associated with a surface of weakly interacting gapless topological surfaces, strong coupling fixed points open the door to explore other possible but distinct gapless boundaries in a topological state.
elements.sent-2411-039: These scale-invariant fixed points, free or strongly interacting conformal ones, therefore can be further applied to classify universal thermodynamics and transport dynamics, either infrared or ultraviolet ones, that one can observe in a physical topological surface state.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our main objective is then to identify all gapless dynamics in a topological surface for a wide class of short-range interactions via an analysis of possible scale symmetric fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-041: Unlike in the standard paradigm where it is believed there shall be a state-boundary correspondence, for the purpose of understanding gapless dynamics, it is more beneficial to have a correspondence between a bulk state and a family of fixed points or conformal field theories supported by a topological bulk, apart from the standard free fermion gapless surface.
elements.sent-2411-042: These emergent conformal fields also represent quantum critical points in critical manifolds that separate the free fermions from other phases of matter.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the following, we will pursue this goal assuming the gapless surface fermions interact via attractive interactions.
elements.sent-2411-044: Before presenting the outline of this article, we want to emphasize that a topological gapless surface can have one-half of a Dirac cone or $N_f=1/2$, unlike in a bulk lattice where one-half of a Dirac cone is strictly forbidden because of the Nielson-Ninomiya theorem of fermion doubling \cite{Nielson(1981),Nielson(1981)(2) .}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Distinct surface states: A consequence of flipping of mass signs
elements.sent-2411-164: Here, we study the bulk topological invariants and examine the underlying physics that leads to distinct surface states for the same topological bulk.
elements.sent-2411-165: We demonstrate that the transition between the distinct surface states is made possible by flipping the fermion mass signs $\delta_{\Gamma_{i }$'s (defined in Eqns.\ref{TI_inv1 ,\ref{TI_inv2 ) at the TRI points on any one of the three $k_a = \pi (a=x,y,z)$ planes in bulk BZ (see Fig.\ref{mass_signs ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: For a 3D lattice, there are eight TRI points in the Brillouin zone.
elements.sent-2411-167: If the lattice is cubic, then we could represent the TRI momenta simply as $\Gamma_{i = (n_1,n_2,n_3) = \pi(n_1 \hat{x + n_2 \hat{y + n_3 \hat{z )$ where $n_i$ can be either 0 or 1.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: From Ref.\cite{fukanemele(2007) the four $Z_2$ invariants $(v_0;v_1,v_2,v_3)$ have the following definition, \begin{subequations \begin{eqnarray}(-1)^{v_0} = \prod_{n_i = 0,1} \delta_{n_1,n_2,n_3}\label{TI_inv1} (-1)^{v_j} = \prod_{n_{(i\neq j)} = 0,1; n_j = 1} \delta_{n_1,n_2,n_3}\label{TI_inv2}\end{eqnarray} \end{subequations where $\delta_{n_1, n_2, n_3}$ can be $\pm 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: For an inversion symmetric system, $\delta_{\Gamma_i}$ is simply the parity eigenvalue of the ground state at the TR-invariant point $\Gamma_i$\cite{fukane(2007) .}
elements.sent-2411-170: It is clear from Eqn.\ref{TI_inv1 that the system is topologically non-trivial for an odd number of negative fermion mass signs $\delta_{\Gamma_{i }$'s; hence, a gapless surface state exists.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: The other three invariants ($v_1,v_2,v_3$) correspond to the product of $\delta_{\Gamma_i}$'s at the four TR-invariant points on the three distinct $k_a=\pi$ planes (here $a=x,y,z$ respectively for $v_1, v_2, v_3$).

(See Eqns.\ref{TI_inv1},\ref{TI_inv2},\ref{srfce_ms} for details).} \label{mass_signs}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Unitary deformation between distinct surface states
elements.sent-2411-190: Here, we shall develop an explicit unitary operator that can smoothly connect the two distinct surface states of a TI with the same bulk topological invariant.
elements.sent-2411-191: We consider the 3D TI to have inversion symmetry so that $\delta_{\Gamma_i}$ (see Eqn.\ref{TI_inv1 ) is just the sign of the mass gap (parity eigenvalue) at the TRI point $\Gamma_{i=(n_1,n_2,n_3)}$.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=4cm, height=3.5cm]{bulkbz.png} \caption{The four TRI points at the $k_z = \pi$ plane in the bulk Brillouin zone (BZ). Also shown are the corresponding mass gaps $m_i(i=1,2,3,4)$ at each of the four TRI points. } \label{bulkbz}
elements.sent-2411-192: We use the effective field theory(EFT) approach to derive the unitary operator.
elements.sent-2411-193: Here, we look at the effective low-energy model of the Hamiltonian given in Eqn.\ref{anisotropicHamiltonian .}
elements.sent-2411-194: By expanding to first order in $k$ around all the time-reversal (TR) invariant points in the bulk Brillouin zone (BZ), only the mass terms in the Hamiltonian persist.
elements.sent-2411-195: Since the effective model only includes TR invariant points from the bulk BZ, it becomes simpler to construct a subspace of the BZ spanned solely by the TR invariant momenta.
elements.sent-2411-196: Consequently, the effective Hamiltonian in momentum space reduces to an $8\times8$ matrix with the diagonal entries corresponding to the respective mass gaps.
elements.sent-2411-197: To deform the surface state, we require the mass to be flipped only on the $k_z = \pi$ plane as discussed in section.\ref{sec:TIdfm B.}
elements.sent-2411-198: So, to look for possible ways to flip the masses, we construct an effective $4\times4$ Hamiltonian matrix in the subspace spanned by the four TRI points in the $k_z = \pi$ plane (see Fig.\ref{bulkbz ).}
elements.sent-2411-199: Then, the effective $4\times4$ Hamiltonian has a diagonal representation as follows: \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}_{\text{eff}} = \beta \otimes \begin{bmatrix} m_1 0 0 0 0 m_2 0 0 0 0 m_3 0 0 0 0 m_4 \end{bmatrix} \label{Heff1}\end{eqnarray} here $m_1, m_2, m_3, m_4$ are the mass gaps at the TRI points $(0,0,\pi)$, $(\pi,0,\pi)$, $(\pi,\pi,\pi)$ and $(0,\pi,\pi)$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-200: In this representation, an off-diagonal element, if present, would imply a coupling between the TRI momentum states in the TI bulk.
elements.sent-2411-201: Now, we shall develop a compact representation for the Hamiltonian to make further computations easier.
elements.sent-2411-202: Let us introduce the Pauli matrices $\Sigma_i (i=x,y,z)$ acting on this subspace.
elements.sent-2411-203: Using this, we shall make the following definitions for the mass matrices $\hat{M _1$ and $\hat{M _2$ as, \begin{subequations \begin{eqnarray}\hat{M}_1 = \frac{1 + \Sigma_z}{2} m_1 + \frac{1 -\Sigma_z}{2} m_2 \hat{M}_2 = \frac{1 + \Sigma_z}{2} m_3 + \frac{1 -\Sigma_z}{2} m_4\end{eqnarray} \end{subequations We define another set of Pauli matrices $\sigma_i (i=x,y,z)$ in this subspace.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: We can recast the Hamiltonian defined in Eqn.\ref{Heff1 in terms of $\Sigma_i$'s and $\sigma_i$'s as, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}_{\text{eff}} = \beta \otimes \left[\frac{ (1 + \sigma_z)}{2} \otimes\hat{M}_1 + \frac{(1 - \sigma_z)}{2}\otimes\hat{M}_2 \right]\label{Heff2}\end{eqnarray} We want to construct a unitary transformation that could flip the signs of the four mass gaps without breaking the time-reversal symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-205: To facilitate this, we define the chirality matrix as, \begin{eqnarray}\gamma = -i \alpha_x \alpha_y \alpha_z\end{eqnarray} which anti-commutes with $\beta$ matrix but commute with the $\alpha_i$ matrices.
elements.sent-2411-206: Applying $\gamma$ alone will flip the sign of mass gaps, but the unitary operator breaks the time-reversal symmetry(TRS).
elements.sent-2411-207: One good strategy is to devise a unitary transformation that realizes coupling between different mass points in the bulk Brillouin zone.
elements.sent-2411-208: For instance, consider the following unitary operation, \begin{equation}U_{1}(\lambda) = \text{Exp}\left[i\frac{\lambda \pi}{2} \sigma_y \otimes \gamma \right]\label{U1}\end{equation} Here, $\sigma_y$ signifies a coupling between the mass points separated by the momentum $K = (\pi, \pi, 0)$.
elements.sent-2411-209: Applying the unitary transformation to the Hamiltonian in Eqn.\ref{Heff2 , we see that the signs of the four mass gaps flip when $\lambda$ approaches one.}
elements.sent-2411-210: However, it simultaneously changes their position too.
elements.sent-2411-211: Since the unitary transformation is equivalent to introducing coupling between the points $k$ and $k + K$ points in the BZ(where $K = (\pi, \pi, 0)$), the mass points separated in the BZ by the vector $K$ will get interchanged when $\lambda$ equals one.
elements.sent-2411-212: Explicitly, the Hamiltonian matrix reads, \begin{eqnarray}U^{-1}_{1}(\lambda = 1)\mathcal{H}_{\text{eff}} U_{1} (\lambda = 1)\nonumber = \beta \otimes \begin{bmatrix} -m_3 0 0 0 0 -m_4 0 0 0 0 -m_1 0 0 0 0 -m_2 \end{bmatrix}\end{eqnarray} The interchange of the mass points is evident in the above Hamiltonian matrix.
elements.sent-2411-213: We fix this by constructing another unitary operator that rotates the mass signs back to their original locations.
elements.sent-2411-214: The unitary operation that can implement this step is, \begin{eqnarray}U_2(\lambda) = \text{Exp}\left[i\frac{\lambda \pi}{2} \sigma_y \otimes I \right]\label{U2}\end{eqnarray} Together, the full unitary operator has the following structure, \begin{eqnarray}U(\lambda) = U^{-1}_2(\lambda)U_{1}(\lambda)\label{U_eft}\end{eqnarray} The effective Hamiltonian after the unitary rotation to $\lambda = 1$ attains the form, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}_{\text{eff}}(\lambda = 1) \nonumber = U^{-1}(\lambda = 1)\mathcal{H}_{\text{eff}}U(\lambda = 1) = \beta \otimes \begin{bmatrix} -m_1 0 0 0 0 -m_2 0 0 0 0 -m_3 0 0 0 0 -m_4 \end{bmatrix}\end{eqnarray} So, one can see that the unitary transformation changes the sign of masses at the four Dirac points.
elements.sent-2411-215: Therefore, to achieve the inversion of mass signs at the four top corners in the Brillouin zone, we initially rotate the effective Hamiltonian with the unitary operator $U_1 (\lambda)$ until $\lambda$ equals one and subsequently rotate it back using $U_2(\lambda)$ to restore the mass points to their initial positions.
elements.sent-2411-216: Notably, for any intermediate value of $\lambda$, the lattice translation and the inversion symmetries are broken.
elements.sent-2411-217: The breaking of translation symmetry is because we introduced the coupling between the momentum states.
elements.sent-2411-218: Furthermore, since $U_1(\lambda)$ contains the $\gamma$ operator (see Eqn.\ref{U1 ), which anti-commutes with the mass term $\beta$, it also breaks the inversion symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-219: However, when $\lambda = 1$, these symmetries are restored.
elements.sent-2411-220: Restoring inversion symmetry is crucial since inversion symmetry is essential for expressing the topological invariants as functions of the parity eigenvalues at each time-reversal invariant (TRI) point.
elements.sent-2411-221: So far, we have obtained the unitary transformation that could deform the TI surface using the effective field theory.
elements.sent-2411-222: Here, we devise a unitary operator that realizes the deformation of the TI surface on a microscopic bulk lattice Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-223: Since the unitary evolution involves coupling between momentum states, we shall use the following representation for the bulk Hamiltonian, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{\hat{H}}_{K}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} \mathcal{H}(k) 0 0 \mathcal{H}(k + K) \end{bmatrix} \label{Hmcrscpc} = \left(\frac{1 + \sigma_z}{2} \right)\mathcal{H}(k) + \left(\frac{1 - \sigma_z}{2} \right) \mathcal{H}(k + K)\nonumber\end{eqnarray} where we fix $K = (\pi, \pi, 0)$.

elements.sent-2411-225: The off-diagonal terms, if present, represent a coupling between momentum states $k$ and $k + K$.
elements.sent-2411-226: We consider the Hamiltonian given in Eqn.\ref{anisotropicHamiltonian .}
elements.sent-2411-227: We shall take an isotropic model by setting $t_x=t_y=t_z = t$.
elements.sent-2411-228: In the above representation, the bulk Hamiltonian takes the form, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{\hat{H}}_{Q}(k) = \sigma_z \left( \alpha_x \sin k_x + \alpha_y \sin k_y \right) + \alpha_z \sin k_z\nonumber + \beta \left(1 - t \cos k_z \right) - \sigma_z \beta \left(t\cos k_x + t\cos k_y \right)\nonumber \label{H0mcrscpc}\end{eqnarray} Recall that while deriving the unitary operator using the effective field theory, we reduced the full $8\times8$ mass matrix to a simple $4 \times 4$ matrix by assuming that the unitary evolution affects only the four TRI points in the $k_z = \pi$ plane(see Eqn.\ref{Heff1 ).}

elements.sent-2411-250: Here, we proposed a possible unitary operation that could smoothly connect the distinct surface states of TI with identical topological bulk.
elements.sent-2411-251: Theoretically, such an adiabatic path should exist because both surfaces are in the same bulk topological phase.
elements.sent-2411-252: We showed here that such a path indeed exists.
elements.sent-2411-253: However, the prize of introducing such a unitary deformation is that it lowers the symmetry of the system.
elements.sent-2411-254: The translation and the inversion symmetry are broken for intermediate values of the parameter $\lambda$ between $0$ and $1$.
elements.sent-2411-255: In addition, the unitary operation introduces infinite-range hopping in the bulk lattice.
elements.sent-2411-256: A practical realization of such a unitary path would be challenging.
elements.sent-2411-257: Therefore, we argue that the number of surface fermion flavors, $\mathcal{N}$, can be stabilized by imposing crystal symmetry constraints.
elements.sent-2411-258: This gives us the confidence to study the effect of strong interactions on the TI surface with $\mathcal{N}$ flavors of surface fermions.
elements.sent-2411-259: Unless the interactions break the symmetries mentioned above or introduce long-range hopping, the number of flavors $\mathcal{N}$ should remain topologically stable.
elements.sent-2411-260: Although we have demonstrated this point in the case of a strong topological insulator (TI), the same principles also apply to a weak TI.
elements.sent-2411-261: Consider the Hamiltonian in Eqn.\ref{anisotropicHamiltonian representing a weak TI phase with two Dirac cones ($\mathcal{N =2$).}}
elements.sent-2411-262: Upon applying the unitary operation $U(\lambda)$, we observe that at $\lambda = 1$, the TI surface retains two Dirac cones.
elements.sent-2411-263: Yet, they are located at complementary points in the surface Brillouin zone (BZ).

elements.subsection-2411-004: Uniqueness of the gapless surface states
elements.sent-2411-270: Before introducing interactions, we emphasize a property of the gapless surface fermions that distinguishes them from the gapless Dirac fermions in a 2D lattice.
elements.sent-2411-271: To begin with, let us define a 'pseudo-chirality' operator as \begin{eqnarray}\Gamma_s = \beta\alpha_z, \,\,\, \Gamma^2_s = 1; \nonumber \left[\Gamma_{s},\alpha_x\right] = [\Gamma_s, \alpha_y]=0.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-272: In the $\alpha-\beta$ representation introduced in this article, $\Gamma_s = \tau_y s_z$, where $\tau_y$ is the Pauli matrix for the orbital part and $s_z$ is the Pauli matrix for spins (see Eqn.\ref{anisotropicHamiltonian ).}
elements.sent-2411-273: The structure of the $\Gamma_s$ operator suggests that the eigenvalue can only be $\pm 1$.
elements.sent-2411-274: We call this term 'pseudo-chirality' because the conventional chirality operator is usually not defined for even space dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-275: The important message here is that the surface fermions of a 3D TI must be $eigenstates$ of this 'pseudo-chirality' operator.
elements.sent-2411-276: Since a strong TI has an odd number of Dirac cones, there would be an odd number of eigenstates for a specific eigenvalue of $\Gamma_s$ operator at a given surface.
elements.sent-2411-277: The majority of the eigenstates would have a specific pseudo-chirality so that the TI surface can be associated with that specific pseudo-chirality value.
elements.sent-2411-278: The location of the surface TRI point at which the Dirac cone is defined is irrelevant here.
elements.sent-2411-279: This is rather an intrinsic property of the topological insulator, disregarding the detailed structure of the surface Brillouin zone.
elements.sent-2411-280: Now let us compare it with a gapless Dirac fermion in a bulk 2D lattice.
elements.sent-2411-281: Each Dirac point will always have both positive and negative pseudo-chiralities.
elements.sent-2411-282: Therefore, a 2D lattice would always have an equal number of eigenstates for each eigenvalue of the $\Gamma_s$ operator.
elements.sent-2411-283: So a bulk 2D lattice cannot be associated with a specific chirality.
elements.sent-2411-284: This is an intrinsic property of lattices due to the well-known fermion-doubling theory\cite{Nielson(1981),Nielson(1981)(2) .}
elements.sent-2411-285: For a weak TI, there is an even number of surface Dirac cones ($\mathcal{N =2$).}
elements.sent-2411-286: In general, there could be an equal number of eigenstates of positive and negative pseudo-chiralities.
elements.sent-2411-287: However, each Dirac cone is uniquely associated with a specific pseudo-chirality.
elements.sent-2411-288: From this perspective, the TI surface fundamentally differs from a bulk 2D lattice when considering individual Dirac cones.
elements.sent-2411-289: However, it is also possible that all (or a majority of) the surface Dirac cones share the same pseudo-chirality eigenvalue, giving the surface a definite pseudo-chirality.
elements.sent-2411-290: Therefore, one can assert that the TI surface is always $distinct$ from the bulk 2D lattice, even if the state is a weak TI.
elements.sent-2411-291: Given this unique property of the topological surface fermions, it would be illuminating to study the effect of interactions between them which would be fundamentally different from the case of a 2D lattice.
elements.sent-2411-292: Here, we shall look at the dynamics of the surface subject to strong, attractive interactions.
elements.sent-2411-293: We are mainly interested in the singlet-pairing interactions that could result in a phase transition to a superconductor.
elements.sent-2411-294: We shall construct an effective 2-dimensional interaction action that could capture the essential low-energy dynamics of the system.

elements.sent-2411-699: Conclusion In this article, we have explored various emergent conformal-field-theory states on surfaces of a 3D topological insulator (cubic crystal) in the presence of strong attractive interactions.
elements.sent-2411-700: Specifically, we considered the cases of interacting TI surfaces with two flavors of 2-component Dirac fermions (weak TI) ($\mathcal{N =2$) and three flavors of 2-component Dirac fermions (strong TI) ($\mathcal{N =3$).}}
elements.sent-2411-701: We itemize the main results below.
elements.sent-2411-702: A) {\bf Phase boundary of Gapless topological surfaces We have constructed effective field theories for interacting surface topological states and identified the phase boundaries of weakly interacting gapless surface fermions via analyzing the renormalization group flow.}
elements.sent-2411-703: The dimension of interaction parameter space $D_p$ where our studies are carried out is defined in terms of the number of surface fermion flavors $\mathcal{N}$ as $D_p=\frac{\mathcal{N (\mathcal{N +1) {2}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-704: The cases of $\mathcal{N =2,3$ have been the focus of the current studies.}
elements.sent-2411-705: In the $\mathcal{N = 2$ weak TI phase with $D_p=3$, we illustrate a two-dimensional conical phase boundary in the three-dimensional parameter space spanned by the interaction matrix elements.}
elements.sent-2411-706: The phase boundary separates the weakly interacting gapless surface fermions from other gapped superconducting phases.
elements.sent-2411-707: At the phase boundary, the universality of generic surface topological quantum critical points is determined by a ring-like co-dimension two conformal manifold or a one-sphere manifold formed by strongly interacting fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-708: When it comes to an $\mathcal{N = 3$ surface with $D_p=6$, the phase boundary becomes a five-dimensional manifold embedded in the six-dimensional interaction parameter space, separating the gapless fermions and gapped superconducting phases.}
elements.sent-2411-709: Generic quantum critical behaviors at the phase boundary are now governed by a co-dimension four conformal manifold, i.e., a two-sphere embedded in the six-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-710: Generally, for a TI surface with attractive interactions, the phase boundary of weakly interacting gapless fermion states is a $D_p-1$-dimensional manifold in the $D_p$-dimensional parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-711: The smooth conformal manifolds where all the renormalization group flows within the phase boundary manifold are channeled into are infrared stable within the $D_p-1$ dimension phase boundary and hence dictate all the universal properties of generic surface topological critical points.
elements.sent-2411-712: B) {\bf The infrared stable conformal manifolds In $D_p-1$ -dimension phase boundaries, there can be multiple conformal manifolds, as well as isolated strong coupling fixed points with very different infrared stabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-713: The infrared stable conformal manifolds mentioned in A), which play the paramount role in our studies of surface topological quantum critical points, are always uniquely characterized by a finite number of marginal operators $n_{MAR}$.
elements.sent-2411-714: The dimension of these smooth manifolds itself is precisely defined by $n_{Mar}$, the number of marginal operators associated with the manifold.
elements.sent-2411-715: In addition, there are always a finite number of infrared irrelevant operators, $n_{IRR}$.
elements.sent-2411-716: However, there shall be only one relevant operator representing the direction perpendicular to the phase boundary so that $n_{IRR +n_{MAR =D_p-1$ for these infrared stable manifolds.}}
elements.sent-2411-717: For $\mathcal{N =2$ weak TI phase with a one-dimensional conformal manifold, we find $n_{IRR =n_{MAR =1$ and $D_p=3$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-718: For $\mathcal{N =3$ strong TI phase, the conformal manifold with $\text{Tr [\hat{V _c]=\epsilon$ has exactly the desired property.}}}
elements.sent-2411-719: It has one single relevant operator and multiple irrelevant and marginal operators: $n_{IRR =3$, $n_{MAR =2$ and $D_p=6$ (See Table.}}
elements.sent-2411-720: Unlike a more standard quantum critical point that can be often associated with a single isolated Wilson-Fisher fixed point, universal properties of generic surface topological phase transitions or quantum critical points appear to be dictated by a lower dimensional conformal manifolds that are embedded in the $D_p-1$ dimension phase boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-721: It is worth mentioning that there appears to be an intimate connection between the geometry of the conformal manifolds in the parameter space and the emergent dynamic symmetries of the RG equation.
elements.sent-2411-722: Assume the symmetry group of the RG equation is $G$ and the invariant subgroup of a fixed point is H, we have found that the corresponding conformal manifold is precisely the coset space of G induced by the subgroup H, i.e. $G/H$ (see Section.\ref{sec:SSB ).}
elements.sent-2411-723: However, this emergent dynamic symmetry group G is sufficient but not necessary for the appearance of the conformal manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-724: In our studies, we also show that conformal manifolds are robust to the effects of certain class of G-symmetry breaking terms, although there can be various distortions.
elements.sent-2411-725: C) {\bf Infrared unstable manifolds and strong coupling fixed points : Apart from the infrared stable manifolds summarized in B), there can be other infrared unstable manifolds as well as isolated strong coupling fixed points (See Table.\ref{tableN2 and Table.\ref{n3table ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-726: The isolated strong coupling fixed points or other manifolds appearing in the phase boundaries turn out to be highly unstable in the limit of infrared, even when RG flows are confined within the $D_p-1$-dimension phase boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-727: In fact, there are multiple relevant operators associated with them, and for that reason, they do not represent generic surface topological quantum critical points.
elements.sent-2411-728: In both the $\mathcal{N =2,3$ case, around the isolated strong coupling fixed points, there are no irrelevant or marginal operators.}
elements.sent-2411-729: That is $n_{IRR =n_{MAR =0$ and all the operators become relevant.}}
elements.sent-2411-730: Therefore, the number of relevant operators in these cases is maximal, equal to $D_p$, the dimension of the interaction parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-731: In the case of $\mathcal{N =3$, there is also another two-sphere manifold ($\text{Tr [\hat{V _c]=2\epsilon$) with three relevant operators instead of a single one.}}}
elements.sent-2411-733: This manifold is also infrared unstable with respect to the flows within the phase boundary.
elements.sent-2411-734: It has three relevant operators, two more than the infrared stable manifold discussed in B).
elements.sent-2411-735: This manifold, together with other strong coupling fixed points, can be identified as a candidate for surface topological multiple critical points rather than generic surface topological quantum critical points.
elements.sent-2411-736: D) We have also studied the stability of these conformal manifolds in the cases of $\mathcal{N =2,3$ against higher loop $SO(\mathcal{N )$-symmetry breaking effects.}}
elements.sent-2411-737: While $SO(\mathcal{N )$-symmetry breaking three-loop one-particle irreducible effects only result in distortions (see Fig.\ref{RGflow_N23loop ), two-loop $SO(\mathcal{N )$-symmetry breaking field renormalization effects can further break the conformal manifolds into isolated fixed points (see Table.\ref{tableN2hghloop or Fig.\ref{RGflow_N22loop ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-738: In the latter case, the manifold can severely constrain the RGE flows between discrete, strong coupling fixed points living in the manifold, leading to an envelope of the flows.
elements.sent-2411-739: The overall stability of these conformal manifolds against $SO(\mathcal{N )$-symmetry breaking effects further depends on how the symmetry is broken.}
elements.sent-2411-740: This emergent symmetry is a sufficient but not necessary condition for the conformal manifold.
elements.sent-2411-741: E) {\bf Emergent boson fields at conformal manifolds At the infrared stable conformal manifolds, we show that the four-fermion interactions are factorizable in the $\mathcal{N}$-dimension flavour space.}
elements.sent-2411-742: Therefore, these conformal manifolds at the phase boundaries can be more conveniently studied via an emergent boson field that interacts with gapless surface fermions (see Eqns.\ref{YkwEFTN2 ,\ref{YkwEFTN3 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-743: In such an effective theory, $\mathcal{N =2,3$ flavors of surface fermions strongly interact with a single flavor of complex boson, resulting in conformal fields in the limit of massless bosons.}
elements.sent-2411-744: The boson mass is directly related to that sole relevant operator associated with the conformal manifolds, orthogonal to the phase boundary in the $D_p$-dimension parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-745: F) {\bf Stability of Fermion degrees of freedom Although the focus of this research project is on interacting gapless fermions, we have also studied the stability of having multiple numbers of Dirac fermions on the surface of non-interacting TI in a cubic crystal.}
elements.sent-2411-746: For instance, a strong TI with a given bulk topological invariant can have either $\mathcal{N =1$ or $\mathcal{N =3$ TI surfaces (see Fig.\ref{phsdgrm ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-747: Questions can be raised about the stability of the respective surface states.
elements.sent-2411-748: We found that the two distinct surfaces can be deformable without closing the bulk gap under the time-reversal symmetry (see Fig.\ref{dfrmtn_TI ).}
elements.sent-2411-749: However, this requires lowering the crystal symmetry of the TI bulk.
elements.sent-2411-750: One has to break translation and inversion symmetries in addition to introducing infinite-range hopping terms to the lattice Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-751: The same set of arguments can also be applied to the weak TI phase.
elements.sent-2411-752: This suggests that the surface with arbitrary $\mathcal{N}$-flavors of 2-component Dirac fermions is stable provided we do not break the crystal's symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-753: The important overall takeaway from our study is that for an interacting TI surface, the number $\mathcal{N}$ of the surface fermions plays a paramount role in setting the phase boundary manifold of the gapless topological surface fermions as well as possible families of emergent conformal fields.
elements.sent-2411-754: The dynamic properties of conformal manifolds emerging in the $D_p-1$ dimension phase boundary are in fact fully determined by $\mathcal{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-755: Only the infrared stable conformal manifolds in the phase boundary can be applied to identify all the possible universality classes of generic surface topological quantum critical points.
elements.sent-2411-756: For instance, we have demonstrated that the $\mathcal{N = 1$ and $\mathcal{N = 3$ TI surfaces, although characterized by the same bulk topological invariants, result in completely different classes of conformal fields at surface topological critical points when strong attractive interactions are present.}}
elements.sent-2411-757: One of the future directions is perhaps to further understand the consequences of the conformal manifolds that we have exclusively associated with the universalities of surface topological quantum critical points.
elements.sent-2411-758: One very unique aspect of conformal manifolds is the existence of multiple marginal operators which define the dimension of the manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-759: It is fascinating to further explore the dynamic implications of these manifolds in the context of surface transport dynamics or hydrodynamics phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-760: Specially at which energy scales can the emergent $SO(\mathcal{N )$ symmetry and resultant manifolds be probed and how does the breakdown of conformal manifolds into the discrete fixed points manifest in infrared physics ? We acknowledge the support of an NSERC(Canada) Discovery Grant under contract No RGPIN-2020-07070 and useful discussions with Julio Parra Martinez and Mark Van Raamsdonk on RGEs and CFTs.. .}
elements.section-2411-010: Derivation of the effective 2D interacting theory from the 3D lattice model
elements.sent-2411-761: In this section, we develop an effective low-energy continuum model for the surface states.
elements.sent-2411-762: We show the derivation explicitly for a specific case of an isotropic cubic lattic with a single Dirac cone surface and then argue that the results can be extended to more general cases of TI surfaces with $\mathcal{N}$ flavors of surface fermions.
elements.sent-2411-763: Consider an isotropic case of the bulk Hamiltonian given in Eqn.\ref{anisotropicHamiltonian , which is when $t_x = t_y = t_z = t$.}
elements.sent-2411-764: In this case, when $1/3 < t < 1$, the TI is in a non-trivial phase and the surface normal to $z$-axis hosts a Dirac cone at the TRI point $Q_1 = (0,0)$.
elements.sent-2411-765: In the bulk BZ, the mass gap is negative at the TRI point $K = (0,0,0)$, while it is positive at all the remaining seven bulk TRI points.
elements.sent-2411-766: In this context, to derive the surface states, we shall expand the bulk Hamiltonian to first order in $k$ about $K$ to get, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}_0 (k) = \vec{\alpha}.\vec{k} + m \beta + \mathcal{O}(k^2) \nonumber\end{eqnarray} where $m_1 = 1 - 3t$ is the bulk energy gap.
elements.sent-2411-767: Now consider the TI surface normal to the $xy$-plane at $z=0$.
elements.sent-2411-768: Since we broke the translation symmetry in $z-$direction, we transform $k_z \rightarrow -i\partial_z$.
elements.sent-2411-769: Also, we write the mass gap as a function of $z$ such that $m(z) = 1 - 3 t (z) = - m$ when $z \rightarrow -\infty$ and $m(z) = + m$ if $z \rightarrow +\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-770: Given that there exists a surface state at $(0,0)$ point in the surface BZ, it means a zero-energy solution exists for the Schrodinger equation at $k_x = k_y = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-771: After some rearrangement, we arrive at the following equation for the surface state, \begin{eqnarray}\left( \frac{\partial}{\partial z} + \Gamma_s m_1 \right) \varphi_1 (0, z) = 0 \label{srfcsteqnappndx}\end{eqnarray} where we define $\Gamma_s = \beta \alpha_z$ and $\varphi_1 (k_x, k_y, z)$ is a 4-component spinor wavefunction.
elements.sent-2411-772: This implies that the surface state if it exists at $z=0$ must be an eigenstate of the operator $\Gamma_s$ with eigenvalue $+1$.
elements.sent-2411-774: Now projecting the Hamiltonian to the two-dimensional space formed by the surface states having positive eigenvalue to the $\Gamma_s$ operator, we arrive at the following effective surface Hamiltonian, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}^{(1)}_{\text{surf}} (k) = s_y k_x - s_x k_y \label{rfcstapndx}\end{eqnarray} The eigenstates will be 2-component spinors.
elements.sent-2411-775: This is exclusively for the low-energy surface fermions in and around the surface TRI point $Q_1 = (0,0)$.
elements.sent-2411-776: The effective Hamiltonians of the surface fermions at the other three TRI points can be derived similarly.
elements.sent-2411-777: The corresponding Lagrangians are given in Eqn.\ref{Lfree in the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-778: Now suppose the bulk topological phase is such that the mass gap is negative at the bulk TRI point $K = (0,0,\pi)$ while positive everywhere else.
elements.sent-2411-779: That means we have to expand the lattice Hamiltonian around the $k_z = \pi$ point.
elements.sent-2411-780: In this case, the Schrodinger equation for the surface states takes the form, \begin{eqnarray}\left( \frac{\partial}{\partial z} - \Gamma_s m_1 \right) \varphi_1 (0, z) = 0\end{eqnarray} On comparing this with Eqn.\ref{srfcsteqnappndx , we see that the surface state, if it exists, must be an eigenstate of the $\Gamma_s$ operator but with eigenvalue $-1$.}
elements.sent-2411-781: The effective surface Hamiltonian will still have the same form as in Eqn.\ref{srfcsteqnappndx , but the difference here is that the pseudo-chirality of the surface state is different.}
elements.sent-2411-782: In this section, we use the dimension reduction method to derive the surface Hamiltonian starting with a bulk interaction theory.
elements.sent-2411-783: We follow closely the path outlined in Ref.\cite{saran(2022) .}
elements.sent-2411-784: Let us begin with a general interaction Hamiltonian for the 3D TI, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}_{\text{I}} = -V\sum_{k,q, p} \sum^{N}_{j=1} c^{\dagger}_{k,z_j} s_y\otimes \hat{I} c^{\dagger}_{q-k,z_j}c_{-p,z_j} s_y\otimes \hat{I} c_{q + p,z_j}\nonumber \label{HBCS}\end{eqnarray} Here the interaction is modelled as on-site in the z-direction.
elements.formula-2411-073: \begin{equation}H_{I} = -V\sum_{k,q, p} \sum^{N}_{j=1} c^{\dagger}_{k,z_j} s_y\otimes \hat{I} c^{\dagger}_{q-k,z_j}c_{-p,z_j} s_y\otimes \hat{I} c_{q + p,z_j}\nonumber \label{HBCS}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-785: The matrix $s_y \otimes \hat{I}$ is to emphasize that the pairing is between the time-reversed partners.
elements.sent-2411-786: The attractive interaction strength $V$ is such that only electronic states within an energy window $[-\Lambda, \Lambda]$ measured from the Dirac point are interacting.
elements.sent-2411-788: If phonons mediate the interaction, then $\Lambda$ becomes the Debye frequency.
elements.sent-2411-789: In the limit of $\Lambda \ll m_i$ for all $i=1,2,3,4$, the bulk fermions is essentially non-interacting.
elements.sent-2411-790: The only particles that lies within this small energy window are the surface fermions if they exist.
elements.sent-2411-791: These surface fermions are defined in terms of the flavor index $i$ which is essentially the location of the surface TRI point $Q_i$.
elements.sent-2411-792: We have developed a domain wall model for the low-energy surface fermions.
elements.sent-2411-793: To write down the effective low-energy model for the interacting Hamiltonian, let us replace the annhilation(creation) operator $c_{k,z_j (c^{\dagger _{k,z_j )$ in the interaction Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H _{I}$ in Eqn.\ref{HBCS with the field operator corresponding to domain wall fermions.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-794: Let $\Psi_i$ be the field operator defined in 3D for the domain wall fermion of flavor $i$.
elements.sent-2411-796: Since we are in the continuoum limit, the sum over the lattice sites in $z$-direction turns out into an integral over $z$ divided by the size $L$ of the material.
elements.sent-2411-797: Let us set that to unity since it is of no consequence to our effective theory.
elements.sent-2411-798: So we finally have, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}^{\text{eff}}_{\text{I}} = - V\sum_{k,p} \sum^{\mathcal{N}}_{i,j = 1} \int dz \,\, \Psi^{\dagger}_{k,i}(z) s_y\otimes \hat{I} \Psi^{\dagger}_{q-k,i}( z)\Psi_{p,j}(z) s_y\otimes \hat{I} \Psi_{p + q,j}(z)\nonumber\end{eqnarray} Here $k,q$ and $p$ are defined up to the limit of respective mass gaps.
elements.formula-2411-074: \begin{equation}H^{eff}_{I} = - V\sum_{k,p} \sum^{N}_{i,j = 1} \int dz \,\, \Psi^{\dagger}_{k,i}(z) s_y\otimes \hat{I} \Psi^{\dagger}_{q-k,i}( z)\Psi_{p,j}(z) s_y\otimes \hat{I} \Psi_{p + q,j}(z)\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-799: That is, $|k| \leq m_i$ and $|p| \leq m_j$.Here $i,j$ runs over the surface Dirac cones.
elements.sent-2411-800: Now one can separate the $z$-dependence out of the spinor field using the relation given in Eqn.\ref{srfcsteqnappndx .}
elements.sent-2411-801: Therefore, this would allow us to write down the interaction Hamiltonian in terms of the 2-component spinor field $\psi_{i (r)$ as, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}^{\text{eff}}_{\text{I}} = -V\sum_{k,q,p} \sum^{\mathcal{N}}_{i,j = 1} \psi^{\dagger}_{k,i} s_y \psi^{\dagger}_{q-k,i} \psi_{-p,j}s_y \psi_{q+p,j} \int dz \,\, f^{2}_{i}(z) f^{2}_{j}(z)\nonumber\end{eqnarray} where $f_{i (z) = \sqrt{m_i e^{- m_i |z|}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-802: This is obtained by solving the Schrodinger equation given in Eqn.\ref{srfcsteqnappndx for the surface states.}
elements.sent-2411-803: Integrating out $z$, we arrive at an effective 2-dimensional theory of attractively interacting surface fermions as, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{H}^{\text{eff}}_{\text{I}} = -\sum_{k,q} \sum^{\mathcal{N}}_{i,j = 1} V_{ij} \psi^{\dagger}_{k,i} s_y \psi^{\dagger}_{q-k,i} \psi_{-p,j}s_y \psi_{q+p,j} \nonumber \label{Hint}\end{eqnarray} where, \begin{eqnarray}V_{ij} = V \frac{m_i m_j}{m_i + m_j}\nonumber\end{eqnarray} where $m_{i}$ and $m_j$ are the bulk mass gaps of the Dirac cones between which the scattering of the singlet pair of fermions occurs due to effective attractive interaction.
elements.section-2411-011: Renormalization group study of the interacting TI surface in $D=2+\epsilon$ dimensions up to two-loop level\label{appndxRGE}
elements.sent-2411-804: Here, we show the derivation of the RG equation for a TI surface with generic $\mathcal{N}$ flavors of attractively interacting surface fermions.
elements.sent-2411-805: First, we identify the Lagrangian shown in Eqns.\ref{Lfree ,\ref{Lfrfmn as the bare Lagrangian with bare fields $\psi_{i,0}$'s and bare couplings $V^0_{ij}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-806: Here we write down the renormalized Lagrangian as follows, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{\mathcal{N}} Z_{\psi_i} \psi^{\dagger}_{i,R} \left[ \partial_\tau + \hat{h}_{1} \right]\psi_{i,R} \nonumber - \mu ^{-\epsilon}\sum^{\mathcal{N}}_{i,j = 1} \sum_{k=1}\tilde{V}^{R}_{ik} Z^V_{kj} \,\,\psi^{\dagger}_{i,R}s_y \psi^{\dagger T}_{i,R} \psi^{T}_{j,R}s_y \psi_{j,R}\end{eqnarray} where $Z_{\psi_i $ is the field renormalization factor of the $i$the flavor of fermion.}
elements.sent-2411-807: Here $\tilde{V ^{R _{ik}$ is the renormalized dimensionless interaction matrix.}}
elements.sent-2411-808: The renormalized action is obtained from the bare action by a simple field rescaling as, $\sqrt{Z_{\psi_i \psi_{i,R = \psi_{i,0}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-809: With this rescaling, we see that the bare and the renormalized interaction strengths are related by \begin{eqnarray}V^0_{ij} = \frac{\mu^{-\epsilon} \sum_{k}\tilde{V}^{R}_{ik} Z^V_{kj}}{Z_{\psi_i} Z_{\psi_j}} \label{RGcond}\end{eqnarray} Note that the LHS is independent of the renormalization scale $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-810: By taking the derivative with respect to $\mu$ on both sides of the above equation, we can derive the RGE of the system.
elements.sent-2411-811: To facilitate discussions on the RGE, we make a few definitions here.
elements.sent-2411-812: From Eqn.\ref{RGcond , we can see that the $bare$ dimensionless interaction matrix element has the following form, \begin{eqnarray}\tilde{V}^0_{ij} = \frac{ \sum_{k}\tilde{V}^{R}_{ik} Z^V_{kj}}{Z_{\psi_i} Z_{\psi_j}}\label{bereintrc}\end{eqnarray} Plugging this back to the RGE in Eqn.\ref{RGcond taking derivative w.r.t $\ln \mu$, we have \begin{eqnarray}- \epsilon \tilde{V}^0_{ij} + \frac{\partial \tilde{V}^0_{ij}}{\partial \ln \mu} = 0\end{eqnarray} Now we use the chain rule to write, \begin{eqnarray}\frac{\partial}{\partial \ln \mu} = \sum_{l,m} \frac{\partial \tilde{V}^R_{lm}}{\partial \ln \mu} \,\,\frac{\partial}{\partial \tilde{V}^{R}_{ml}} = \sum_{l,m} \beta_{lm} \frac{\partial}{\partial \tilde{V}^{R}_{ml}}\end{eqnarray} Plugging this back, we get, \begin{eqnarray}\sum_{l,m} \beta_{lm} \frac{\partial \tilde{V}^0_{ij}}{\partial \tilde{V}^{R}_{ml}} = \epsilon \tilde{V}_{ij}\label{btafunc}\end{eqnarray} From this equation, one can extract the beta function very easily.}}

elements.sent-2411-869: It has been known that SPT surfaces can be identified via the method of gauge anomalies (or vice versa, see Ref.\cite{wen(2013) ) as the lattice symmetry can be implemented in a non-local way on surfaces.}
elements.sent-2411-870: For given anomalies, one can identify phases of surfaces depending on whether spontaneous symmetry breaking(SSB) or topological orders (TO) further appear on a surface.
elements.sent-2411-871: The surfaces of 3D bosonic SPT states and quantum criticality and dynamics have been thoroughly studied in Ref.\cite{ashvin(2013) .}
elements.sent-2411-872: We refer the readers to those discussions.
elements.sent-2411-873: The current studies are fully consistent with this general paradigm of topological surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-874: What we have addressed here is that when interactions are attractive pairing ones, the classes of CFTs that appear on topological surfaces can be further directly related to bulk invariants, which we have used to infer the number of surface fermions.
elements.sent-2411-875: For instance, for strong TIs (with cubic crystal symmetry), the CFTs are either related to an isolated SUSY fixed point or a conformal manifold with its tangent space set by two marginal operators but without SUSY.
elements.sent-2411-876: These are the general results within a given class of interacting topological surfaces rather than within a given broader class of anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-877: Our family of gapless states are therefore a subclass with further inputs of interactions.
elements.sent-2411-878: They are related to quantum phase transitions to SSB states that break the protecting charge $U(1)$ symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-879: There can also be other fascinating transitions, such as SSB to SSB states and TO to TO states.
elements.sent-2411-880: They are very interesting to look further into in connection to conformal manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-881: In our current studies, we have restricted ourselves to quantum phase transitions between the well-observed gapless free fermions phase and SSB states breaking the charge $U(1)$ symmetries with interaction-parameter inputs, in addition to topological data.
elements.sent-2411-882: Our main focus is to understand the phase boundary that separates the gapless phase and SSB states, breaking one of the protecting symmetries and what we can have on the phase boundary manifold.
elements.sent-2411-883: We have dedicated section.\ref{sec:cfnml exclusively for the discussions on the conformal manifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-884: There, we discuss certain features of the conformal manifold that appear at the phase boundary of the gapless surface fermions.
elements.sent-2411-885: We focus on two aspects: 1) relations to generic conformal manifolds discussed in quantum field theories and 2) its distinction from conventional isolated fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-886: Conformal manifolds Previously, we mentioned that the phase boundary manifold of the $\mathcal{N =2, 3$ flavor TI surfaces contains conformal sub-manifolds with co-dimensions $D_{co = 1, 4$ respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-887: These conformal manifolds observed at the surface quantum criticality require special attention.
elements.sent-2411-888: In this section, we shall describe certain generic features of the conformal manifold, especially on how it is distinct from a conventional isolated fixed point.
elements.sent-2411-889: Following this, we list down the special properties of the conformal manifold observed in our studies.
elements.sent-2411-890: A conformal manifold is a family of interacting Hamiltonians connected by exactly marginal operators.
elements.sent-2411-891: For instance, let $\mathcal{S _c$ be an action defined at a point on the conformal manifold.}
elements.sent-2411-892: Let $\{\mathcal{O ^{i _M \} (i=1,..n)$ be the exactly marginal operators at the given point on the manifold.}}
elements.sent-2411-893: Then, after a marginal deformation, \begin{eqnarray}\mathcal{S}_{c} + \delta \mathcal{S} = \mathcal{S}_c + \sum^{n}_{i=1}\int d^{D} x \,\delta \lambda^i_M \mathcal{O}^i_M(x)\end{eqnarray} the theory remains conformal invariant.
elements.sent-2411-894: Here, $\delta \lambda^{i _M$ is the infinitesimal marginal coupling away from the fixed point corresponding to the marginal operator $\mathcal{O ^{i _{M}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-895: The number of marginal operators determines the dimension of the conformal manifold.
elements.sent-2411-896: One important property of these exactly marginal operators is that their scaling dimension $\triangle_{\mathcal{O _M = D$, the spacetime dimensions of the theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-897: In contrast, the canonical scaling dimensions of $\mathcal{O _{M}$ is $2D-2$.}
elements.sent-2411-898: Eqns.\ref{mrgnlprtsN2 and \ref{mrgnlprtsN3 provide the detailed expressions for the marginal operators $\mathcal{O ^i_M (x)$ of the conformal manifolds that appears on the phase boundary of $\mathcal{N =2$ and $\mathcal{N =3$ TI surfaces respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-899: Before proceeding further and starting our discussions on our results, we want to remark on the nature of the specific conformal manifolds discussed in this article and their relations to more generic conformal manifolds, which are well discussed in modern quantum field theories.
elements.sent-2411-900: As discussed above, typical construction of conformal manifolds usually starts with a set of exact marginal operators that form a tangent space of a manifold and can then be applied to generate other distinct conformal field theories via deformations.
elements.sent-2411-901: Practical implementations of such procedures either require primary conformal operators with a known scale dimension matching the space-time dimension as in $(1+1)D$, or supersymmetry conformal operators to ensure such exact marginal operators as in a higher dimension.
elements.sent-2411-902: The best well-known high-dimension example is the N=4 supersymmetric Yang-Mills theory\cite{seiberg(1988),seiberg(1994) , which can support un-compact conformal manifolds with logarithm divergent diameters measured using the standard Zamodolchikov metric\cite{zamolodchikov(1986) .}}
elements.sent-2411-903: The tangent space of our manifolds can be defined by marginal operators discussed in Appendix \ref{apndxsclngdmnsns when they become exactly marginal in the limit of our interests.}
elements.sent-2411-904: Our manifolds can then be constructed in a similar way via deformations.
elements.sent-2411-905: However, in this case, deformations simply lead to theories within the same universality class because of the higher symmetry of our manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-906: The manifold is either a one-sphere with a uniform metric or a two-sphere with a uniform curvature tensor, i.e. they form representations of $SO(2)$ and $SO(3)$ groups, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-907: The deformation of original theories induces a rotation in the interaction parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-908: Because of the emergent $SO(\mathcal{N )$ dynamic symmetry in RGEs discussed below, the operator scaling dimensions are invariant under rotations, hence the deformations.}
elements.sent-2411-909: Therefore, the deformations, although resulting in a family of distinct interaction Hamiltonians, do not result in a new universality class.
elements.sent-2411-910: The conformal manifold in this article only hosts a single universality class, far fewer classes than the generic ones discussed before.
elements.sent-2411-911: For this reason, we name it a featureless conformal manifold to distinguish it from more generic cases, such as the ones in SCFTs, where each point on a manifold actually defines a distinct class of CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-912: It shall be emphasized here that our result is distinctly different from an isolated Wilson-Fisher fixed point, which can be considered a zero-sphere.
elements.sent-2411-913: An isolated fixed point forbids exact marginal operators as otherwise, it again can be deformed and induce a smooth manifold.
elements.sent-2411-914: So, from this point of view, the key distinction between a smooth manifold, as discussed in our case, and an isolated point, a more standard critical point, is the existence of a tangent space at each point of the manifold and exact marginal operators, which usually define the metric of the manifold.
elements.sent-2411-915: And the number of exact marginal operators defines the dimension of the manifold just like in all other general discussions.
elements.sent-2411-916: Then, we shall look for unitary operations that could connect these distinct surface states.
elements.sent-2411-917: We find two non-local unitary operators that are equivalent to infinite-depth ones.
elements.sent-2411-918: The first one (Eqn.\ref{U1_ltce ) does change the bulk invariant, as anticipated, therefore connecting two topologically different TIs, which is not surprising given the general paradigm about topological bulks.}
elements.sent-2411-919: The second one (Eqn.\ref{U_efl_tce ), however, does not change the bulk, though of infinite depth, but changes the surface Dirac cones.}
elements.sent-2411-920: The main purpose of this part of the discussion is to understand how stable the surface Dirac cones are, given that for a given bulk, there are two different surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-921: Our analysis shows that although they are associated with the same bulk, for a given topological bulk, there appears to be no other way to deform one surface into the other surface unless it is unitary with infinite depth.
elements.sent-2411-922: Here, we have constructed the second type of infinite depth unitary that changes the surface of Dirac cones without changing the bulk.
elements.sent-2411-923: Specifically, we find that our unitary accomplishing this necessarily partially breaks protecting symmetries and induces long-range hopping.
elements.sent-2411-924: This strongly implies that when restricted to local unitary when all protecting symmetries are enforced, the surface states remain distinct and topologically stable, reinforcing our focus on the criticality of a fixed number $\mathcal{N}$ of Dirac fermions.
elements.sent-2411-925: This is further illustrated in Fig.\ref{dfrmtn_TI .}
